title_en: The Prophet's Clothing, Food, and Drink
content_id:
Syama'il mendedikasikan beberapa bab untuk pakaian, makanan, dan minuman Nabi — bukan untuk mengangkat hal-hal biasa, melainkan karena para Sahabat memahami bahwa bahkan dalam detail-detail ini, Nabi menjadi teladan suatu cara hidup. Pilihan-pilihan beliau dalam bidang-bidang ini tidak asketik hingga titik ketegasan juga tidak berlebihan dalam arah mana pun, dan para ulama mengambil panduan yang kaya dari riwayat-riwayat yang dilestarikan tentangnya.

Dalam berpakaian, Nabi menyukai kesederhanaan. Beliau mengenakan pakaian dari katun dan wol, dan warna putih paling sering disebutkan sebagai preferensinya. Beliau juga mengenakan kain bergaris dari Yaman — kain yang dihargai karena kualitasnya — dan ada riwayat tentang beliau mengenakan warna hijau. Beliau tidak mengenakan sutra murni, yang diharamkan bagi laki-laki, meskipun beliau menerima pakaian sutra sebagai hadiah dan mendistribusikannya kepada orang lain. Cincinnya dari perak dengan batu akik Habsyi, dikenakan di tangan kanan menurut beberapa riwayat dan di tangan kiri menurut yang lain, dan di atasnya terukir 'Muhammad — Rasulullah,' yang beliau gunakan sebagai cap resminya pada surat-surat kepada para penguasa.

Kebiasaan makannya sederhana. Beliau tidak makan sambil bersandar. Beliau makan dengan tangan kanan. Beliau menyebut nama Allah sebelum makan dan memuji Allah setelah selesai. Beliau tidak pernah mengkritik makanan: jika menyukainya, beliau memakannya, dan jika tidak menyukainya, beliau hanya meninggalkannya tanpa komentar. Prinsip ini — tidak mengkritik makanan — dicatat oleh Abu Hurairah sebagai praktik yang konsisten sepanjang hidupnya.

Makanan yang paling beliau sukai termasuk kurma, yang mungkin beliau makan sendiri sebagai makanan lengkap. Beliau menyukai semangka dan memakannya dengan kurma segar. Beliau menyukai madu. Di antara daging-dagingan, beliau memakan daging domba dan lebih menyukai paha. Tharid — hidangan dari kuah dan roti yang diremukkan — digambarkan dalam berbagai riwayat sebagai di antara makanan yang beliau puji. Beliau makan roti dengan minyak atau cuka ketika makanan yang lebih rumit tidak tersedia. Beliau tidak selalu memiliki roti sama sekali: Aisyah melaporkan berbulan-bulan berlalu tanpa sesuatu pun yang menyalakan api masak mereka kecuali kurma dan air.

Dalam minuman, beliau meminum air dan madu, dan dilaporkan meminum campuran kurma yang direndam dalam air — nabidz — sebelum difermentasi, yang merupakan perkara yang dibahas para ulama secara rinci mengenai hukumnya. Beliau tidak minum sambil berdiri, menurut hadis yang ditafsirkan oleh sebagian ulama sebagai rekomendasi kuat dan oleh yang lain sebagai larangan. Beliau bernafas di luar wadah saat minum, mengangkat cangkir dari mulutnya di antara tegukan alih-alih minum dalam satu tegukan panjang.

Mungkin dimensi paling mencolok dari bab-bab ini adalah apa yang mereka ungkapkan tentang keadaan rumah tangga Nabi. Beliau adalah pemimpin negara yang berkembang, menerima delegasi dari raja-raja dan memimpin pasukan. Namun rumah tangganya hidup pada tingkat kesederhanaan material yang kesulitan ditandingi oleh banyak Sahabatnya yang paling miskin sekalipun. Aisyah menggambarkan kasur Nabi sebagai sepotong kulit yang diisi serat. Beliau bisa tidak makan selama beberapa hari. Beliau wafat dengan baju besinya yang dijadikan jaminan pada seorang laki-laki Yahudi untuk sejumlah gandum. Syama'il tidak membingkai ini sebagai kekurangan. Ia membingkainya sebagai pilihan — pilihan seorang manusia yang hubungannya dengan dunia ini telah secara fundamental diubah oleh pengetahuannya tentang apa yang ada di baliknya.
content_en:
The Shama'il devotes multiple chapters to the Prophet's clothing, food, and drink — not to elevate the mundane but because the Companions understood that even in these details, the Prophet modeled a way of living. His choices in these areas were neither ascetic to the point of severity nor indulgent in any direction, and the scholars drew rich guidance from the narrations preserved about them.

In clothing, the Prophet favored simplicity. He wore garments of cotton and wool, and the color white is mentioned most frequently as his preference. He also wore striped Yemeni cloth — a fabric prized for its quality — and there are narrations of him wearing green. He did not wear pure silk, which is forbidden for men, though he received silk garments as gifts and distributed them to others. His ring was silver with an Abyssinian carnelian stone, worn on the right hand according to some narrations and the left according to others, and on it was engraved 'Muhammad — Messenger of Allah,' which he used as his official seal on letters to rulers.

His food habits were modest. He did not eat while reclining. He ate with the right hand. He mentioned the name of Allah before eating and praised Allah after finishing. He would not criticize food: if he liked something, he ate it, and if he did not like something, he simply left it without remark. This principle — not criticizing food — was noted by Abu Hurayrah as a consistent practice across his life.

His preferred foods included dates, which he might eat alone as a meal. He loved watermelon and would eat it with fresh dates. He enjoyed honey. Among meats, he ate lamb and preferred the shoulder. Tharid — a dish of broth and broken bread — is described in multiple narrations as among the foods he praised. He ate bread with oil or vinegar when more elaborate food was not available. He did not always have bread at all: Aisha reported months would pass in which nothing was lit on their cooking fire except dates and water.

In drink, he drank water and honey, and he is reported to have drunk a mixture of dates soaked in water — nabidh — before it fermented, which is a matter scholars have discussed in detail regarding permissibility. He did not drink while standing, according to a hadith that some scholars interpret as a strong recommendation and others as a prohibition. He would breathe outside the vessel while drinking, taking the cup from his mouth between sips rather than drinking in one long draft.

Perhaps the most striking dimension of these chapters is what they reveal about the household circumstances of the Prophet. He was the leader of a growing state, receiving delegations from kings and commanding armies. Yet his household lived at a level of material simplicity that many of his poorest Companions struggled to match. Aisha described the Prophet's mattress as a piece of leather stuffed with fiber. He would go without food for days. He died with his armor pledged as security with a Jewish man for a quantity of barley. The Shama'il does not frame this as deprivation. It frames it as choice — the choice of a man whose relationship to this world had been fundamentally reoriented by his knowledge of what lay beyond it.
content_ar: The Shama'il devotes multiple الأبواب to النبي ﷺ's clothing, food, and drink — not to elevate the mundane but لأن the الصحابة understood that even in these details, النبي ﷺ modeled a الطريق of living. His choices in these areas were neither ascetic to the point of severity nor indulgent in any direction, and the العلماء drew rich الهداية from the narrations preserved about them. In clothing, النبي ﷺ favored simplicity. He wore garments of cotton and wool, and the color white is mentioned most frequently as his preference. He أيضاً wore striped Yemeni cloth — a fabric prized for its quality — and there are narrations of him wearing green. He did not wear pure silk, which is forbidden for men, though he received silk garments as gifts and distributed them to others. His ring was silver with an Abyssinian carnelian stone, worn on the الحق hand وفقاً لـ some narrations and the left وفقاً لـ others, and on it was engraved 'محمد ﷺ — Messenger of الله,' which he used as his official seal on letters to rulers. His food habits were modest. He did not eat بينما reclining. He ate with the الحق hand. ذكر the name of الله قبل eating and praised الله بعد finishing. He would not criticize food: إذا he liked something, he ate it, and إذا he did not like something, he simply left it without remark. This principle — not criticizing food — was noted by أبو هريرة as a consistent practice across his الحياة. His preferred foods included dates, which he might eat alone as a meal. He loved watermelon and would eat it with fresh dates. He enjoyed honey. Among meats, he ate lamb and preferred the shoulder. Tharid — a dish of broth and broken bread — is described in multiple narrations as among the foods he praised. He ate bread with oil or vinegar عندما more elaborate food was not available. He did not always have bread at all: Aisha reported months would pass in which nothing was lit on their cooking fire except dates and water. In drink, he drank water and honey, and he is reported to have drunk a mixture of dates soaked in water — nabidh — قبل it fermented, which is a matter العلماء have discussed in detail فيما يخص permissibility. He did not drink بينما standing, وفقاً لـ a الحديث that some العلماء interpret as a strong recommendation and others as a prohibition. He would breathe outside the vessel بينما drinking, taking the cup from his mouth between sips rather than drinking in one long draft. Perhaps the most striking dimension of these الأبواب is what they reveal about the household circumstances of النبي ﷺ. He was the leader of a growing state, receiving delegations from kings and commanding armies. Yet his household lived at a level of material simplicity that many of his poorest الصحابة struggled to match. Aisha described النبي ﷺ's mattress as a piece of leather stuffed with fiber. He would go without food for days. He died with his armor pledged as security
